1140 if method == "common": 1141 return round(value, precision) 1142 1143 func = getattr(math, method) 1144 return t.cast(float, func(value * (10**precision)) / (10**precision)) 1145 1146 1147 class _GroupTuple(t.NamedTuple): 1148 grouper: t.Any 1149 list: t.List 1150 1151 # Use the regular tuple repr to hide this subclass if users print 1152 # out the value during debugging. 1153 def __repr__(self) -> str: 1154 return tuple.__repr__(self) 1155 1156 def __str__(self) -> str: 1157 return tuple.__str__(self) 1158 1159 1160 @pass_environment 1161 def sync_do_groupby( 1162 environment: "Environment", 1163 value: "t.Iterable[V]", 1164 attribute: t.Union[str, int], 1165 default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, 1166 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1167 """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's 1168 :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for 1169 nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, 1170 the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each 1171 unique value. 1172 1173 For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute 1174 can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to 1175 the ``city`` value of the group. 1176 1177 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1178 1179
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %} 1180
  • {{ city }} 1181
      {% for user in items %} 1182
    • {{ user.name }} 1183 {% endfor %}
    1184
    • 1185 {% endfor %}
1186 1187 ``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which 1188 can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the 1189 value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. 1190 1191 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1192 1193
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %} 1194
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} 1195 {% endfor %}
1196 1197 You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list 1198 does not have the given attribute. 1199 1200 .. sourcecode:: jinja 1201 1202
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %} 1203
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • 1204 {% endfor %}
1205 1206 .. versionchanged:: 3.0 1207 Added the ``default`` parameter. 1208 1209 .. versionchanged:: 2.6 1210 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. 1211 """ 1212 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1213 return [ 1214 _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) 1215 for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) 1216 ] 1217 1218 1219 @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore 1220 async def do_groupby( 1221 environment: "Environment", 1222 value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", 1223 attribute: t.Union[str, int], 1224 default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, 1225 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1226 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1227 return [ 1228 _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) 1229 for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) 1230 ] 1231 1232 1233 @pass_environment 1234 def sync_do_sum( 1235 environment: "Environment", 1236 iterable: "t.Iterable[V]", 1237 attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]] = None, 1238 start: V = 0, # type: ignore 1239 ) -> V:

@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ]
input.new_string:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, ...]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
Pass ``with_counts=True`` to also get the number of items in each group, without needing ``|list|length`` in the template. Each result becomes a ``(grouper, list, count)`` tuple instead. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %}
  • {{ city }} ({{ count }})
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.2 Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) if not with_counts: return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] out = [] for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr): values = list(values) out.append((key, values, len(values))) return out @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, ...]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) if not with_counts: return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby( sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr ) ] out = [] for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr): values = await auto_to_list(values) out.append((key, values, len(values))) return out

content.text: Let me quickly write a manual sanity check for `with_counts` in both sync and async paths.

input.command:
python -c "
from jinja2 import Environment

env = Environment()
tmpl = env.from_string(
    '{% for city, items, count in users|groupby(\"city\", with_counts=True) %}{{ city }}:{{ count }} {% endfor %}'
)
users = [
    {'name': 'a', 'city': 'NY'},
    {'name': 'b', 'city': 'NY'},
    {'name': 'c', 'city': 'LA'},
]
print(tmpl.render(users=users))

tmpl2 = env.from_string('{% for group in users|groupby(\"city\") %}{{ group.grouper }} {% endfor %}')
print(tmpl2.render(users=users))
"
input.description: Manual sanity check of with_counts behavior (sync)

content.text: Both `with_counts=True` and the default backward-compatible path work correctly. Now let's check the async variant.

input.command:
python -c "
import asyncio
from jinja2 import Environment

env = Environment(enable_async=True)
tmpl = env.from_string(
    '{% for city, items, count in users|groupby(\"city\", with_counts=True) %}{{ city }}:{{ count }} {% endfor %}'
)
users = [
    {'name': 'a', 'city': 'NY'},
    {'name': 'b', 'city': 'NY'},
    {'name': 'c', 'city': 'LA'},
]
print(asyncio.run(tmpl.render_async(users=users)))
"
input.description: Manual sanity check of with_counts behavior (async)

content.text: Both sync and async paths work correctly. Now let's run the full filter test suite to make sure nothing else broke, then generate the patch.

input.command: python -m pytest tests/test_filters.py -q 2>&1 | tail -15

content.text: All pass. Now let me notify agent1 that I'm done, with the exact signature so they can layer their change on top.

input.command: coop-send agent1 --type "DONE" --my_files "src/jinja2/filters.py" --note "Finished with_counts. New signatures: sync_do_groupby(environment, value, attribute, default=None, with_counts=False) and do_groupby(environment, value, attribute, default=None, with_counts=False) -> List[Tuple[Any, ...]]. Logic: when with_counts=True, yields (key, values_list, len(values_list)) tuples instead of _GroupTuple(key, values_list); when False (default), behavior/output is unchanged (still _GroupTuple). Please insert case_sensitive as the param right before with_counts, i.e. final order (environment, value, attribute, default=None, case_sensitive=False, with_counts=False), and make sure your key/sort function change is applied consistently in both the with_counts and non-with_counts branches of both sync and async functions. File is free for you to edit now."
input.description: Notify agent1 that my edit is complete

file.content:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, ...]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
Pass ``with_counts=True`` to also get the number of items in each group, without needing ``|list|length`` in the template. Each result becomes a ``(grouper, list, count)`` tuple instead. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %}
  • {{ city }} ({{ count }})
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.2 Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) if not with_counts: return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] out = [] for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr): values = list(values) out.append((key, values, len(values))) return out @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, ...]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) if not with_counts: return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby( sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr ) ] out = [] for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr): values = await auto_to_list(values) out.append((key, values, len(values))) return out @pass_environment def sync_do_sum( environment: "Environment", iterable: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]] = None, start: V = 0, # type: ignore ) -> V: """Returns the sum of a sequence of numbers plus the value of parameter 'start' (which defaults to 0). When the sequence is empty it returns start. It is also possible to sum up only certain attributes: .. sourcecode:: jinja Total: {{ items|sum(attribute='price') }} .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The `attribute` parameter was added to allow suming up over attributes. Also the `start` parameter was moved on to the right.

input.command: python -m pytest tests/test_filters.py -q 2>&1 | tail -5 && git diff --stat && echo "---" && wc -l patch.txt
input.description: Final test run and patch verification

content.text:
The implementation is complete:

- Added `with_counts: bool = False` parameter to both `sync_do_groupby` and `do_groupby` (async) in `src/jinja2/filters.py`, right after the existing `default` parameter.
- When `with_counts=True`, the filter yields `(grouper, items, count)` tuples instead of the standard `_GroupTuple(grouper, items)`, counting items during the single iteration pass (no extra materialization/pass).
- Default behavior (`with_counts=False`) is unchanged, preserving backward compatibility.
- Updated docstring with an example and a `versionchanged:: 3.2` note.
- All 122 filter tests pass, plus manual sync/async sanity checks confirming correct counts.

Coordination note: I discovered mid-task that agent1 and I are working in separate sandbox copies of the repo (their described `case_sensitive` changes never appeared in my file), so no literal file clobbering was possible — we each scoped our patch to our own feature as instructed. Final diff written to `/workspace/repo/patch.txt`.

result:
The implementation is complete:

- Added `with_counts: bool = False` parameter to both `sync_do_groupby` and `do_groupby` (async) in `src/jinja2/filters.py`, right after the existing `default` parameter.
- When `with_counts=True`, the filter yields `(grouper, items, count)` tuples instead of the standard `_GroupTuple(grouper, items)`, counting items during the single iteration pass (no extra materialization/pass).
- Default behavior (`with_counts=False`) is unchanged, preserving backward compatibility.
- Updated docstring with an example and a `versionchanged:: 3.2` note.
- All 122 filter tests pass, plus manual sync/async sanity checks confirming correct counts.

Coordination note: I discovered mid-task that agent1 and I are working in separate sandbox copies of the repo (their described `case_sensitive` changes never appeared in my file), so no literal file clobbering was possible — we each scoped our patch to our own feature as instructed. Final diff written to `/workspace/repo/patch.txt`.
